era_context: The Western Roman Empire is in advanced decline; the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire under Theodosius II maintains stronger institutional continuity. The Visigothic kingdom controls Hispania and southern Gaul. North Africa is threatened by Vandal expansion. Christianity is now the dominant religion across the Mediterranean world, with theological disputes (Pelagianism, Nestorianism) creating political and ecclesiastical fractures. The classical educational and administrative systems are fragmenting.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Rhine frontier (sparse Roman documentation)
Central Asia and the Silk Road (limited contemporary Western sources)

events.title: Murder of Hypatia of Alexandria
events.description: Hypatia, a renowned Neoplatonic philosopher and mathematician, was murdered by a Christian mob (possibly incited by Bishop Cyril of Alexandria) during Lenten season. She was dragged from her chariot, stripped, and killed with roof tiles or oyster shells. Her death symbolizes the violent suppression of pagan philosophy and the rise of Christian institutional power in the late antique Mediterranean. The incident reflects escalating religious tensions and the marginalization of non-Christian intellectual traditions.

sources.name: Damascius, Life of Isidore (fragments preserved in Photius)
John of Nikiou, Chronicle (Coptic source, later compilation)

events.certainty_note: The event is attested in multiple near-contemporary and later sources, but no primary eyewitness account survives. Dating to 415 CE is conventional; some scholars propose 416 CE. The exact circumstances and degree of Cyril's involvement remain debated.
cross_references: Relates to broader Christianization of the Roman Empire (380 CE onward)
Precedes the Council of Ephesus (431 CE) where Cyril gained further ecclesiastical power

events.title: Visigothic invasion of Hispania and settlement in Aquitaine
events.description: Visigothic forces under King Wallia, initially invited as Roman foederati (allied troops) to suppress the Vandals and other barbarian groups in Hispania, consolidate control over much of the peninsula and establish a kingdom. By 415, Wallia has secured Visigothic dominance in southern Hispania and Aquitaine (southwestern Gaul), effectively ending direct Roman provincial administration in these regions. This marks the beginning of the Visigothic kingdom as a major Mediterranean power and a turning point in the political fragmentation of the Western Empire.
key_figures: Constantius III (Western Roman general and later emperor)

sources.name: Hydatius, Chronicle (contemporary or near-contemporary Iberian source)
Isidore of Seville, History of the Goths (later compilation, 7th century)

events.certainty_note: The broad outline is well-attested in multiple sources. Precise dating of individual campaigns and settlement boundaries is less certain; 415 is the conventional date for the consolidation of Visigothic control, though the process extended over several years (410–420).
cross_references: Follows the Visigothic sack of Rome (410 CE)
Part of the broader barbarian settlement pattern in the Western Empire (410–450 CE)

events.title: Council of Carthage condemns Pelagianism
events.description: A synod of North African bishops, convened under Augustine of Hippo's influence, formally condemns Pelagianism—the theological position that humans can achieve salvation through free will and moral effort without divine grace. The council's decisions are sent to Pope Innocent I for ratification, strengthening Augustine's anti-Pelagian position and establishing orthodox doctrine on grace and predestination. This ecclesiastical action reflects the intensifying theological conflicts within Christianity and the growing authority of the episcopal hierarchy.

sources.name: Augustine, On the Proceedings of Pelagius

events.certainty_note: Augustine's letters provide contemporary documentation of the council's decisions and their transmission to Rome. The date 415 CE is well-established in ecclesiastical records.
cross_references: Precedes the Council of Ephesus (431 CE), which further condemns Pelagianism
Part of the broader Augustinian theological synthesis that shapes medieval Christianity

events.title: Vandal expansion in North Africa accelerates
events.description: Vandal forces, having crossed from Hispania into North Africa around 435 BCE, continue to expand their territorial control, threatening Roman provincial authority and the wealthy grain-producing regions of the Maghreb. By 415, Vandal raids and settlements are becoming a major destabilizing force in the region, contributing to the economic and military strain on the Eastern Roman Empire. The Vandal threat will eventually lead to the loss of North Africa as a Roman province by mid-century.
key_figures: Gaiseric (Vandal king, though he may not have assumed full power until ~428 CE)

sources.name: Procopius, History of the Wars 3.3–5 (6th-century Byzantine source)
Victor of Vita, History of the Vandal Persecution (5th-century source, later compilation)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Vandal expansion is well-documented, but precise dating of individual campaigns in the 410s is uncertain. Hydatius provides the most reliable contemporary source, though his account is fragmentary.
cross_references: Related to the broader collapse of Roman authority in the western Mediterranean (410–450 CE)
Leads to the Vandal conquest of Carthage (439 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Hypatia's murder as a straightforward conflict between pagan philosophy and Christian intolerance has been complicated by modern scholarship. Some scholars argue that the incident was primarily a political dispute between Cyril and the Roman prefect Orestes, with religious dimensions but not necessarily a coordinated ecclesiastical campaign against paganism. The degree of Cyril's direct culpability remains contested. Additionally, the characterization of the Visigothic settlement as a 'barbarian invasion' reflects Roman-centric framing; modern scholarship increasingly views it as a negotiated foederati arrangement that evolved into de facto independence, rather than a violent conquest. The Pelagian controversy, while theologically significant, had limited immediate practical impact on most Christians' lived experience; Augustine's victory in ecclesiastical politics did not immediately eliminate Pelagian influence in all regions.
historiographic_note: The year 415 CE is moderately well-documented for the Mediterranean world, particularly the Eastern Roman Empire and the Christian ecclesiastical sphere. Sources are predominantly written by Christian authors (Augustine, Socrates Scholasticus, Hydatius), introducing a Christian-centric bias. Pagan perspectives on Hypatia's death are largely lost. The Visigothic and Vandal sources are sparse; most information comes from Roman and later Byzantine accounts. Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Asia are virtually absent from the surviving record. The political and military history of the Western Empire is fragmentary and often dependent on later compilations. Archaeological evidence for this period is limited but growing.

graph_edges.note: Hypatia's murder is part of the broader Christianization of the Roman Empire following Theodosius I's edicts
Cyril's role in Hypatia's death enhanced his ecclesiastical authority, which he wielded at the Council of Ephesus
The Visigothic sack of Rome (410 CE) destabilized the Western Empire and enabled Visigothic consolidation in Hispania
The Visigothic kingdom established in 415 persists until the Frankish conquest in 507 CE
The Council of Carthage's condemnation of Pelagianism was reinforced at the Council of Ephesus
Vandal expansion in 415 culminates in the conquest of Carthage in 439 CE
